query: role of CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ regulatory T cells in regulating immune response

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, T-Cell Lymphocyte -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The last major type of T cells is the T regulatory cell (also known as suppressor T cells). These cells modulate immune responses and inhibit autoimmune processes (such as autoreactive immune cells). T regulatory cells produce inhibitory IL-10 and IL-35 cytokines to control immune responses, as well as using CTLA-4 to inhibit B7 on antigen-presenting cells to decrease immune responses. Production of T regulatory cells is spurred by cytokine IL-2 (so much so that in autoimmune disease IL-2 is often absent) and TGF-beta. T regulatory cells express the TCR (CD3), CD4 (they likely share a common lineage with CD4+ Helper T cells), CTLA-4 and CD25. They also express the biomarker FOXP3 (a transcription marker important in their development and immune functions). There has been a recent interest in T regulatory cells as a method to promote wound healing after surgery and to battle cancers. [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Allergy Immunotherapy -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Several cellular and molecular mechanisms explain the beneficial effects of immunotherapy including allergic specific suppression of inducible CD4(+), CD 25+, forkhead box p3+ T-regulatory cells, and IL-10 secreting T-regulatory cells, preventing their increase in peripheral blood. Other mechanisms include suppression of eosinophils, mast cells, and basophils, and the switching of antibodies from IgE to IgG4 blocking antibodies. [9] [10] [11] Other mechanisms, including a switch from a TH2 to a THI  immune deviation,  and other changes in humoral/cellular immunity, are still being elucidated. [10] [12] [13] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Immune Response -- Function -- Regulation of Immune Responses
retrieved_docs.content: Regulatory T cells: Suppress immune responses, maintain tolerance to self-antigens, and prevent autoimmune diseases. Negative feedback mechanisms: Cytokines such as IL-10 and transforming growth factor beta inhibit the activation of immune cells. Checkpoint proteins: Molecules such as CTLA-4 and PD-1 on T cells can downregulate immune responses, a target for certain cancer immunotherapies.

retrieved_docs.title: Interleukin -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Gene knockout mouse studies have provided evidence that the primary IL-2 function in vivo is the suppression of T responses. Mice lacking IL-2 or its receptor (CD25) develop lymphadenopathy and T cell-mediated autoimmunity.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: CD4 helper T cells: Facilitate cell-to-cell interactions and cytokine release to activate and control immune and inflammatory responses

retrieved_docs.title: X-Linked Immunodeficiency -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: IPEX occurs as a result of a mutation in the FOXP3 transcription factor. FOXP3 is crucial for the function of Treg cells also known as regulatory T lymphocyte. Treg cells have a strong immune suppressive effect and serve a function in establishing immune balance. Moreover, they are involved in transplantation tolerance as well as in the regulation of autoimmune and allergic events.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: TH0: The initial role of activated CD4 T cells, promotes cell immunity by activating dendritic cells and stimulating lymphocyte growth; releases cytokines IL-2, IL-4, and IFN-gamma; can develop into TH1, TH2, TH17, or other TH cells

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, T-Cell Lymphocyte -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: T-cell function is dependent on the type of T cell. The major T cells are CD8+ T cells, CD4+ T cells, and T regulatory cells (i.e., suppressor T cells). Several other minor types of T cells exist that are beyond this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Cyclosporine -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanism of action of cyclosporine is as a calcineurin inhibitor, a cytochrome P450 3A4 inhibitor, and a P-glycoprotein inhibitor. Cyclosporin A (CsA) inhibits the synthesis of interleukins (IL), including IL-2, which is essential for the self-activation of T lymphocytes (LT) and their differentiation. Cyclosporine is effective due to specific and reversible inhibition of immunocompetent lymphocytes in the G0 and G1-phase of the cell cycle. The T-helper cell is the primary target, although it may also suppress T-suppressor cells. The LT-B-lymphocyte (LB) co-operation is essential for activation of LB; the latter also gets inhibited. In addition, research has demonstrated that CsA had an inhibiting effect on CD4+ CD25+ Tregs, which might block the host immune tolerance potentiality. Metabolism: Via hepatic CYP3A4 and is metabolized into a pair of hydroxylated derivatives (AM1 and AM9) and one N-methylated derivative (AM4N). Enzymes inhibited: CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, T-Cell Lymphocyte -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: The other structural markers of T cells depend on the T-cell type in question. CD4+ T cells have CD4, whereas CD8+ T cells have CD8 as an added co-receptor. T regulatory cells have CD4, and CD25 added as co-receptors for their TCRs. As mentioned above, T cells have numerous other biochemical receptors on their surface, and these largely depend on the T cell's function. [1] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Immune Response -- Related Testing -- Blood Lymphocyte Subpopulations
retrieved_docs.content: Total lymphocyte count T lymphocytes (CD3+, CD4+, and CD8+) B lymphocytes (CD19 and CD20) CD4+ to CD8+ ratio

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: Dendritic cells (antigen-presenting cells): Facilitate activation of an antigen-specific response by the innate system; present antigens via major histone complexes to activate CD8 and CD4 T cells

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Immune Response -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Idiopathic CD4+ lymphocytopenia

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: MHC II: Found on antigen-presenting cells that interact with CD4 T cells; responsible for presenting exogenous antigens

retrieved_docs.title: Latent Autoimmune Diabetes -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Regulatory T lymphocytes produce proteins like transcription factor forkhead box protein 3 (FOXP3), suppressing autoimmunity. Hypermethylation of DNA and decreased expression of FOXP3 diminish protection against immune destruction. FOXP3 and other proteins are lower in the high GADA subtype than the low GADA, indicating higher β-cell destruction in the former. [20] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Vaccine (Vaccination) (Archived) -- Mechanism of Action -- Blood Lymphocyte Subpopulations
retrieved_docs.content: Total lymphocyte count T lymphocytes (CD3, CD4, and CD8) B lymphocytes (CD19 and CD20) CD4/CD8 ratio

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 381
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants comprise a diverse group of agents that are used to dampen immune activity in settings such as organ transplantation, autoimmune disorders, and inflammatory diseases. This class includes inhibitory interleukin agents, the antimetabolite mycophenolate (also known as mycophenolic acid) and its pro‑drug mycophenolate mofetil (including its hydrochloride form), as well as azaserin, basiliximab, cyclosporine, everolimus, gliotoxin, muromonab‑CD3, and tacrolimus. Mycophenolate and mycophenolic acid function by blocking purine synthesis in lymphocytes, whereas cyclosporine and tacrolimus inhibit calcineurin, preventing activation of T‑cell transcription factors. Everolimus exerts its effect through inhibition of the mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) pathway, limiting cell proliferation. Basiliximab and muromonab‑CD3 are monoclonal antibodies that target specific surface receptors on activated T cells, providing targeted immunosuppression. Gliotoxin, a fungal toxin, also possesses immunosuppressive properties through interference with cytokine signaling. Collectively, these agents illustrate the multiple mechanistic pathways—interleukin inhibition, antimetabolite activity, calcineurin blockade, mTOR inhibition, and monoclonal antibody‑mediated receptor blockade—by which immunosuppressants achieve therapeutic immune modulation, underscoring their central role in contemporary patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 687
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants represent a broad class of agents that dampen immune activity through a variety of mechanisms and are employed in transplantation, autoimmune disorders, and other conditions requiring immune modulation. The group includes inhibitory interleukin agents, antimetabolites such as mycophenolate, mycophenolate mofetil, mycophenolate mofetil hydrochloride, mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová), azaserin, and koformycin, which interfere with nucleotide synthesis and lymphocyte proliferation. Calcineurin inhibitors, exemplified by cyklosporiny (cyclosporine) and takrolimus (tacrolimus), block T‑cell activation by inhibiting calcineurin signaling. The mTOR pathway is targeted by everolimus, providing another route to suppress cell growth and immune responses. Monoclonal antibodies and biologic agents such as basiliximab, daklizumab (daclizumab), muromonab‑CD3, and elipticiny act on specific surface receptors or cytokine pathways to prevent lymphocyte activation. Polyclonal preparations like antilymfocytární sérum (antilymphocyte serum) also contribute to immunosuppression by broadly neutralizing immune cells. Glucocorticoids, including meprednisone and paramethason (paramethasone), exert anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects by altering gene transcription. Additionally, gliotoxin, a fungal toxin, possesses immunosuppressive properties through inhibition of NF‑κB and other signaling cascades. Together, these diverse agents illustrate the multiple biochemical targets—interleukin signaling, calcineurin, mTOR, nucleotide synthesis, surface receptors, and transcriptional pathways—through which immune function can be attenuated, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate regimens and managing associated risks.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1817
retrieved_docs.content: Prednisone, rimexolone, and paramethasone are all members of the class of systemic glucocorticoids. Systemic glucocorticoids constitute a group of steroid medications that are administered throughout the body to exert anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects. By being part of this class, each of these agents shares the common pharmacologic properties of glucocorticoids, including modulation of gene expression, reduction of cytokine production, and suppression of immune cell activity, which are leveraged in the treatment of a wide range of inflammatory and autoimmune conditions. Understanding that prednisone, rimexolone, and paramethasone belong to the systemic glucocorticoid category helps clinicians select appropriate therapy, anticipate similar side‑effect profiles, and apply consistent monitoring strategies across these related drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1846
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic glucocorticoids constitute a class of corticosteroid drugs that exert broad anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive actions throughout the body. Both prednison and rimexolone are individual agents that belong to this systemic glucocorticoid class, sharing the common pharmacologic properties of glucocorticoid receptor activation, suppression of cytokine production, and modulation of immune cell function. Recognizing prednison and rimexolone as members of the systemic glucocorticoid group helps clinicians understand their therapeutic indications, dosing considerations, and potential side‑effect profiles, which are characteristic of systemic glucocorticoid therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1485
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants constitute a broad therapeutic class used to dampen immune activity in settings such as organ transplantation, autoimmune disorders, and inflammatory diseases. This class includes agents that act through diverse mechanisms, ranging from inhibition of interleukin signaling to blockade of calcineurin or mTOR pathways and direct depletion of lymphocytes. Representative members of the immunosuppressant family are inhibitory interleukinu (agents that inhibit interleukin activity), mycophenolate, and mykofenolát mofetil (mycophenolate mofetil). Additional compounds classified as immunosuppressants are antilymfocytární sérum (antilymphocyte serum), azaserin, basiliximab, cyklosporiny (cyclosporine), daklizumab (daclizumab), elipticiny, everolimus, gliotoxin, and hydrochlorid mykofenolátu mofetilu (the hydrochloride form of mycophenolate mofetil). Specific immunosuppressive drugs also highlighted within this group are kyselina mykofenolová (mycophenolic acid), muromonab‑CD3, and takrolimus (tacrolimus). By recognizing the wide array of immunosuppressants and their distinct pharmacologic actions, clinicians can tailor immunomodulatory regimens to achieve optimal graft survival, control autoimmune activity, and minimize adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1422
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants are a pharmacologic class that suppresses or modulates the immune system and are employed in organ transplantation, autoimmune disorders, and other conditions requiring immune inhibition. Within this class, specific subcategories include inhibitory interleukins, mycophenolate, and mycophenolate mofetil (mykofenolát mofetil). A wide range of agents are classified as immunosuppressants: antilymfocytární sérum (antilymphocyte serum), basiliximab, cyklosporiny (cyclosporine), daklizumab, elipticiny, everolimus, gliotoxin, and hydrochlorid mykofenolátu mofetilu (mycophenolate mofetil hydrochloride). Additional members of the immunosuppressant group are koformycin, kyselina mykofenolová (mycophenolic acid), muromonab‑CD3, and takrolimus. Together, these drugs share the common function of dampening immune activity, though they differ in mechanisms—ranging from inhibition of interleukin signaling to blockade of calcineurin pathways or depletion of lymphocyte populations—providing clinicians with multiple therapeutic options tailored to specific clinical scenarios.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1971
retrieved_docs.content: Glucocorticoids, Systemic constitute a class of potent anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive agents, and the drugs prednison, meprednisone and paramethason are members of this systemic glucocorticoid group. As systemic glucocorticoids, these medications share mechanisms of action that involve binding to intracellular glucocorticoid receptors, modulating gene transcription, and reducing the production of pro‑inflammatory cytokines. Consequently, they are employed in the management of a wide range of inflammatory, autoimmune, and allergic conditions, as well as in certain oncologic and endocrine disorders. Understanding that prednison, meprednisone and paramethason belong to the Glucocorticoids, Systemic class helps clinicians anticipate their therapeutic effects, potential side‑effect profiles, and appropriate dosing strategies across diverse patient populations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1594
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants represent a diverse group of agents that inhibit immune activity and are employed in transplantation, autoimmune disorders, and other conditions requiring immune modulation. This class includes mycophenolate and its prodrug mycophenolate mofetil, as well as the hydrochloride form of mycophenolate mofetil. Antilymfocytární sérum (antithymocyte serum), azaserin (azathioprine), basiliximab, cyklosporiny (cyclosporine), daklizumab (daclizumab), and everolimus are also classified as immunosuppressants. Additionally, kyselina mykofenolová (mycophenolic acid) and muromonab‑CD3 belong to the same therapeutic category. Together, these drugs provide clinicians with multiple mechanisms to achieve targeted immunosuppression for improved patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1734
retrieved_docs.content: Antilymfocytární sérum, azaserin, basiliximab, cyklosporiny, daclizumab, everolimus, gliotoxin, koformycin, kyselina mykofenolová, muromonab‑CD3 and takrolimus are all classified as immunosuppressive agents. These drugs share the common function of dampening immune system activity, which makes them valuable in settings such as organ transplantation, where preventing graft rejection is essential, and in the treatment of certain autoimmune disorders. Antilymfocytární sérum and muromonab‑CD3 act as antibodies that target lymphocytes, while basiliximab, daclizumab and takrolimus inhibit T‑cell activation through distinct molecular pathways. Cyklosporiny and everolimus belong to the calcineurin‑inhibitor and mTOR‑inhibitor families, respectively, providing synergistic suppression of cellular immunity. Azaserin, gliotoxin, koformycin and kyselina mykofenolová (mycophenolic acid) exert their effects by interfering with nucleic acid synthesis or other intracellular processes critical for lymphocyte proliferation. Together, this diverse group of immunosuppressants offers clinicians multiple mechanisms to tailor therapy according to the specific immunologic needs of each patient, thereby improving graft survival and disease control while balancing the risk of infection and other adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2127
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants constitute a diverse group of agents that dampen immune activity and are employed in transplantation, autoimmune diseases, and other conditions requiring immune modulation. This class includes inhibitory interleukin compounds, the antimetabolite mycophenolate and its pro‑drug mycophenolate mofetil, antilymphocyte serum, azaserin, the monoclonal antibodies basiliximab and daclizumab, the mTOR inhibitor everolimus, and the fungal toxin gliotoxin. Additional members are cyclophosphamide (koformycin), mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová), the glucocorticoid paramethasone, and the calcineurin inhibitor tacrolimus. These drugs act through various mechanisms—such as blocking interleukin signaling, inhibiting nucleotide synthesis, antagonizing the IL‑2 receptor, inhibiting mTOR signaling, or modulating glucocorticoid and calcineurin pathways—to achieve immunosuppression and improve patient outcomes in settings where controlling the immune response is critical.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2247
retrieved_docs.content: Everolimus, kyselina mykofenolová (mycophenolic acid), meprednisone, paramethason, and takrolimus are all members of the immunosuppressant drug class. As immunosuppressants, each of these agents functions to dampen the activity of the immune system and is commonly employed in settings such as organ transplantation and the treatment of autoimmune disorders. Although they share the overarching purpose of immune suppression, they act through distinct mechanisms: everolimus inhibits the mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) pathway, takrolimus (tacrolimus) blocks calcineurin, kyselina mykofenolová interferes with inosine monophosphate dehydrogenase to reduce lymphocyte proliferation, and both meprednisone and paramethason are corticosteroids that modulate inflammatory gene expression. Recognizing that these diverse compounds belong to the immunosuppressant category aids clinicians in selecting appropriate therapeutic regimens, anticipating drug‑specific side effects, and optimizing patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1460
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants constitute a broad pharmacologic class that includes a diverse array of agents used to attenuate immune system activity in settings such as organ transplantation, autoimmune disorders, and inflammatory diseases. Within this class are inhibitory interleukinu compounds, which target interleukin pathways to reduce immune activation, and mycophenolate, a purine synthesis inhibitor that limits lymphocyte proliferation. Antilymfocytární sérum, a polyclonal antibody preparation, also belongs to the immunosuppressant group, as do azaserin and basiliximab, both of which interfere with T‑cell activation. Cyklosporiny (cyclosporine) and takrolimus (tacrolimus) are calcineurin inhibitors that suppress cytokine production, while daklizumab (daclizumab) blocks the IL‑2 receptor to prevent T‑cell expansion. Additional members include elipticiny, gliotoxin, and the pro‑drug hydrochlorid mykofenolátu mofetilu (mycophenolate mofetil hydrochloride), which is metabolized to the active agent mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová). Koformycin, a nucleoside analog, and muromonab‑CD3, a monoclonal antibody directed against the CD3 complex, further expand the repertoire of immunosuppressive therapies. Collectively, these agents illustrate the extensive range of mechanisms—such as cytokine inhibition, lymphocyte depletion, and signal transduction blockade—through which immunosuppressants achieve therapeutic immune modulation, underscoring their critical role in contemporary patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1514
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants are agents that modulate or suppress the immune response and are classified as a subset of the broader category immunologické faktory. Within this class, inhibitory interleukinu is identified as a specific child entity. A variety of pharmaceutical and biological compounds are recognized as immunosuppressants, including antilymfocytární sérum, azaserin, basiliximab, cyklosporiny, daklizumab, elipticiny, gliotoxin, koformycin, and muromonab‑CD3. Each of these agents exerts immunosuppressive effects through distinct mechanisms, ranging from monoclonal antibody targeting of T‑cell receptors (e.g., basiliximab, muromonab‑CD3) to calcineurin inhibition (e.g., cyklosporiny) and cytokine inhibition (e.g., inhibitory interleukinu). Understanding the hierarchical relationship—immunosuppressants as a child of immunologické faktory and encompassing the listed agents—provides a comprehensive framework for selecting and applying immune‑modulating therapies in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2184
retrieved_docs.content: Immunization for autoimmune disease includes human vaccination as a subordinate concept, indicating that human vaccination is regarded as a specific form of immunization employed to prevent or modulate autoimmune disease in humans.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 609
retrieved_docs.content: Deflazacort, prednison, rimexolone, and paramethason are all members of the therapeutic class identified as Glucocorticoids, Systemic, which falls under the broader category of Immunological Agents [TC]. As systemic glucocorticoids, each of these agents possesses anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties that are utilized in the treatment of various immune‑mediated and inflammatory conditions. Their classification within Immunological Agents [TC] highlights their role in modulating immune responses, and recognizing them as part of the same systemic glucocorticoid group aids clinicians in understanding their similar mechanisms of action, potential side‑effect profiles, and therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1630
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic glucocorticoids constitute a pharmacologic class of corticosteroid agents that are administered throughout the body to exert anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects. Representative members of this class include deflazacort, rimexolone, and paramethasone (also referred to as paramethason). By sharing the core glucocorticoid structure, these drugs act on glucocorticoid receptors to modulate gene transcription, reduce cytokine production, and suppress immune cell activation, making them useful in the treatment of autoimmune diseases, allergic reactions, and various inflammatory disorders. Recognizing that deflazacort, rimexolone, and paramethasone are all members of the systemic glucocorticoid category helps clinicians select appropriate agents based on potency, pharmacokinetics, and side‑effect profiles, thereby optimizing patient care.
